Skip to contents
STcompare 0.1.0
  • Install
    • Getting Started with STcompare
    • Acute Kidney Injury 10x Visium
    • Comparison of MERFISH and Visium for Mouse Brain
  • Functions

matchingVariograms

matchingVariograms.Rd

Function to create a permutation of spatial dataset X by starting with a randomly shuffled version of X and then smoothing it until the variogram of this permutation matches the variogram of the unmodified X

Usage

matchingVariograms(
  X.randomized,
  long,
  lat,
  delta,
  target_variog,
  prctile,
  ids,
  i,
  seed = 0
)

Arguments

X.randomized

numeric vector random permutation of the values of X across its x,y-coordinates.

long

numeric vector: y-coordinates of points in dataset X

lat

numeric vector: x-coordinates of points in dataset X

delta

numeric vector Given a point in dataset X, the percentage of neighbors which should be within the smoothing kernel. This can be a list or a single numeric. Input to `locfit::lp` as `nn`

target_variog

variogram variogram of dataset X that will be used as target when doing the matching. See geoR::variog

prctile

numeric: the max distance that should be used for evaluating the fit between the variograms from the permuted data and the target variogram. Can be determined from calculating all distances between the points and then calculating a specified quantile. Input to `geoR::variog` as `max.dist`

ids

integer: indices from 1 to the number of points in the dataset X

i

integer the number indicating the ith permutation

seed

integer Seed for the random number generator. Default 0.

Value

The output is returned as a list.

  • residusnumeric vector of length of delta, sum of squares of the residuals between the target variogram and the permuation variogram for each delta

  • delta.star.idinteger, the index of the delta that minimizes the residual sum of squares

  • hat.X.delta.starnumeric vector of length of X.randomized, permutation generated from optimal delta, whose variogram best matches the variogram of X

References

adapted from: Viladomat, Júlia et al. “Assessing the significance of global and local correlations under spatial autocorrelation: a nonparametric approach.” Biometrics vol. 70,2 (2014): 409-18. doi:10.1111/biom.12139

Examples


data(quakes)

#remove duplicated positions
quakes_data <- quakes[!duplicated(cbind(quakes$lat, quakes$long)),]

lat <- quakes_data$lat
long <- quakes_data$long
X <- quakes_data$depth
ids <- 1:length(X)

#number of permutations
B <- 3

#sequence of deltas
delta <- seq(0.1,0.9,0.1)

# maximum distance for the variogram set at the 25% percentile of
# the distribution of pairs of distances:
dists <- dist(cbind(lat, long))
prctile <- quantile(dists, probs = 0.25)

# variogram of variable X that will be used as target when doing the matching:
target_variog <- geoR::variog(data = X, coords = cbind(long, lat),
                              max.dist = prctile, option = "bin",
                              messages = FALSE)
#> Warning: no display name and no $DISPLAY environment variable

# create B random permutations of the values of X across locations:
X.randomized <- lapply(1:B, function(i) {
   sample(X, size = length(X), replace = FALSE)
})

# create B permutations of the values of X with variograms that match the
#target variogram of X:
output <- lapply(1:B, function(i) {
  output <- matchingVariograms(X.randomized[[i]], long, lat,
                               delta, target_variog, prctile, ids, i)
})

output
#> [[1]]
#> [[1]]$residus
#> [1] 986946227  77599218  11904997  24474093  23120332  13986652  21741995
#> [8]  24084522   3471635
#> 
#> [[1]]$delta.star.id
#> [1] 9
#> 
#> [[1]]$hat.X.delta.star
#>   [1] 26548.82 26571.45 26671.98 26243.09 26547.79 26256.29 27657.46 26729.82
#>   [9] 26761.65 26424.63 26668.10 27522.97 26408.14 26608.03 27562.47 25983.03
